Patch Set 1:

I have a feeling that this will break some use cases.  The current policy is that the node with the highest ctime is in control of the mtime. In some cases, such as writes to an OST update ctime+mtime, then update the MDS, we don't want the MDS ctime update to overwrite the mtime of the OST.

It still doesn't fix the problem that the MDS clock should not be used for setting the timestamps, which I think should be fixed regardless of what else is done.  Removing the MDS clock from the scene will reduce the number of places where a bad timestamp could be come from.

I think the most important use case to keep correct is that of a single client.  All the other cases with multiple clients or bad clocks could cause problems if the clocks are out of sync, so there is no way to fix that problem without having in-sync clocks.

Patch Set 1:

Note there are a few bugs in this area that are worthwhile to read:
https://bugzilla.lustre.org/show_bug.cgi?id=11063
https://bugzilla.lustre.org/show_bug.cgi?id=21489

Patch Set 1: Verified+1

Tests received by maloo, run on CentOS release 6.5/x86_64: (https://testing.hpdd.intel.com/test_sessions/087f9836-9c04-11e4-a352-5254006e85c2).  Ran 7 tests.  No failures.

Patch Set 1:

Tests received by maloo, run on CentOS release 6.5/x86_64: (https://testing.hpdd.intel.com/test_sessions/8155ad6c-9c1e-11e4-b679-5254006e85c2).  Ran 16 tests.  No failures.

Patch Set 1:

"I have a feeling that this will break some use cases. The current policy is that the node with the highest ctime is in control of the mtime. In some cases, such as writes to an OST update ctime+mtime, then update the MDS, we don't want the MDS ctime update to overwrite the mtime of the OST."

Do you mean the close case in SOM? If SOM is what your concern, then we can just add some special check on the epoch close of SOM.

If you are referring a metadata operation after write, then ctime update won't overwrite mtime, unless it's an utime operation, in that case, mtime should be overwritten.


"It still doesn't fix the problem that the MDS clock should not be used for setting the timestamps, which I think should be fixed regardless of what else is done. Removing the MDS clock from the scene will reduce the number of places where a bad timestamp could be come from."

This patch fixed one of the place that MDS time being used: the setfacl case I mentioned in SGI-64. Once we discovered other places, we'd fix it in other patches.


"I think the most important use case to keep correct is that of a single client. All the other cases with multiple clients or bad clocks could cause problems if the clocks are out of sync, so there is no way to fix that problem without having in-sync clocks."

Indeed, I think what best we can do is: always use the times from client to update timestamps.

Patch Set 5: Code-Review+1

Patch Set 5: Code-Review-1

I'm definitely OK with the change to the ldiskfs code, and it would make sense to split this into a separate patch so that it can land independently.

Oleg and I have a bit of concern about the MDS code changes because this may result in the timestamps changing forward and back if two clients have slightly different clocks, rather than moving continuously forward as they should.

I agree that it could be problematic if one client clock is quite wrong compared to another (e.g. hours/days/years), so we might consider to add a "sanity check" on the ctime so that it doesn't go totally wrong?  That should probably be done in a separate patch, however.

I also added Vitaly for review, since I recall he was working on several problems in this area in the past, and maybe he has a better memory about any subtle bugs.

Patch Set 5:

"Oleg and I have a bit of concern about the MDS code changes because this may result in the timestamps changing forward and back if two clients have slightly different clocks, rather than moving continuously forward as they should."

We don't have such kind of check on OSTs, so the mctime of OST objects could go back too (on truncate operation) 

I'd consider that (changing ctime forward & back) as desired behavior, rather than keeping ctime unchanged when the file is touched by the client with lagged time.

Patch Set 6:

Honestly speaking, I am still some afraid of there may be some dangerous of allowing client to set the file's ctime arbitrarily. Is it possible to offer some parameter on the MDT side to allow the administrator to control that. For example, "ctime_reverse". In fact, we have controlled atime via "atime_diff".

Patch Set 6:

On local posix filesystem, the ctime can be set arbitrarily, why should we add this restriction for Lustre?

Patch Set 6:

Lustre does not strict POSIX, just like we cannot make the "atime" to be updated as local FS does.

Patch Set 6:

Where can ctime be set arbitrarily?  The api from userspace only allows setting mtime and atime: http://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man2/utimensat.2.html

Patch Set 6:

Andreas, to be clear, what we talked difference between Lustre and local fs is: when admin change system time to past, then further file operations will set ctime to the new system time for local fs, however, for Lustre, the ctime won't change because the file's original ctime > current system time.

Sorry for the confusion on 'arbitrarily'.

Patch Set 6: Code-Review-1

This change cannot be cherry-picked to master.
Please rebase the change locally and upload the rebased commit for review.

Abandoned

Abandon the patch because of concerns on side effect.

parents.subject: LU-3536 osd: allocate it for each iteration.

commit.subject: LU-6137 mdd: update timestamps arbitrarily
commit.message:
LU-6137 mdd: update timestamps arbitrarily

There was a timestamps update policy on MDS: ctime is increasing
only, which means if the ctime to be updated is behind of current
inode->i_ctime, then this timestamps update will be rejected.

This policy sounds not reasonable and it could lead to following
problems:

1. If time isn't synced on clients, the file created by the client
   having advanced clock will have advanced ctime, so any operations
   from the client with lagging clock won't be able to update file
   timestamps.

2. If the client clock is adjusted to a past time, then this client
   won't be able to change timestamps to the already create files.

3. Server time could be used on inode->i_ctime if the client time
   isn't provided for certain operation from some old version
   client, and if the server time is ahead of client time, we'll
   run into the same problem.

4. Sometime ldiskfs API may set server time on inode->i_ctime (
   ext4 nomctime patch doesn't cover all cases), and it should be
   overwritten by client time in upper layer (see
   mdt_reint_setxattr()), however, because of the timestamps update
   policy, the overwrite operation could fail if the server time
   is ahead of client time.

This patch removes this policy and make the timestamps can be
updated arbitrarily.

Test-Parameters: alwaysuploadlogs \
mdtfilesystemtype=ldiskfs mdsfilesystemtype=ldiskfs \
ostfilesystemtype=ldiskfs testlist=posix

Signed-off-by: Niu Yawei <yawei.niu@intel.com>
Change-Id: I0fc73b4418cec863dc5b052f3677bc64389e00d8
